serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Squid Pepper Fry is approximately 150 grams, often enjoyed as an appetizer or side dish alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) and cooked seafood composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients are adapted from USDA FoodData Central squid (raw, FDC ID 175168) with upward adjustment for fat content reflecting coconut oil used in flash-frying. Sodium adjusted to reflect added salt typical of the preparation. This is the closest approved-source equivalent; no exact entry for Squid Pepper Fry (Koonthal Milagu Varuval) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Squid Pepper Fry. GI is estimated at 20 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked seafood dishes as documented in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Squid contains negligible starch; the 3.4 g/100 g carbohydrate derives primarily from spices and shallots. Coconut oil fat further attenuates any glycemic response. This estimation basis is consistent with peer-reviewed GI literature for protein-dominant seafood preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt during preparation
hypertension.guidance: At 290 mg sodium per 100 g, individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced salt when ordering, as sodium can accumulate quickly when the dish is served alongside other salty accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low estimated glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 20, Squid Pepper Fry produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions without high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol content and saturated fat from coconut oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid contributes approximately 180 mg cholesterol per 100 g and the dish contains saturated fat from coconut oil; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit servings to 100 g and choose preparations using minimal oil to keep saturated fat intake within clinical targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Squid Pepper Fry, known in Malayalam as Koonthal Kurumulaku Varthathu and in Tamil as Kanava Milagu Varuval, is a bold, high-heat seafood preparation from the coastal regions of Kerala and Tamil Nadu in South India. Tender calamari rings are flash-fried in coconut oil with freshly crushed black pepper as the dominant spice, alongside shallots, curry leaves, and a touch of fennel. The squid is cooked only until it turns opaque, preserving a soft, succulent texture beneath a dark, aromatic spice coating. The dish delivers an intensely peppery, savory flavour with subtle sweetness from shallots and the herbal fragrance of curry leaves. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (16.2 g/100 g), low in carbohydrates (3.4 g/100 g), and has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 20, making it suitable for blood sugar management. It is a rich source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. Sodium content (290 mg/100 g) and dietary cholesterol (180 mg/100 g) are clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is commonly served as an appetizer or side dish at lunch or dinner in South Indian coastal seafood restaurants.
common_user_queries: How to make Koonthal Milagu Varuval?
Is Squid Pepper Fry healthy for diabetics?
Nutritional value of calamari pepper fry
Kanava Milagu Varuval recipe and nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central squid data (closest approved-source equivalent) with adjustments for cooking oil and seasoning. No exact entry for this prepared dish exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central squid (mixed species, raw, FDC ID 175168) with fat content increased to reflect coconut oil absorption during flash-frying, and sodium adjusted for added salt typical of this preparation. Saturated fat (2.5 g), monounsaturated fat (2.0 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.5 g), and water (68.0 g) are estimated from USDA squid data adjusted for cooking method; trans fat is 0.0 g consistent with coconut oil and no hydrogenated fat use. Cholesterol (180 mg) is consistent with USDA squid values. No exact Squid Pepper Fry entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, or ICMR-NIN databases; this is the closest approved-source adaptation. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood category per Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care. Input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan'; corrected to non-vegetarian/non-vegan as squid is an animal product. Portions deduplicated from four identical entries to one canonical serving of 150 g.
